rows.5. Which session or moment was most valu: The closed working group farm to FIM session provided both space and a little more time (still not enough for relationship and coalition building) to connect with farmer stakeholders. This was important because I am part of a community of farmers and all that we have accomplished together is rooted in shared values and trust. Connecting with and knowing that there was at least one other community-led farmer-led helped my colleague and me to feel that we had another person in the room who understood our holistic perspective of fresh locall food access and farming.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro:
Some ideas to consider for the future:
1- hold the national convening every 3 years or so and make space for regional gatherings centered in strategic action implementation.
2- de-politicize the gatherings even more so by not including figures like Dr. Oz. I say this because he made very political comments that were not necessary and also not proven (like noting fraud and graft, etc.). There was no sense that he was there in community with the people doing the work. There were, however, other people in government (for example, a 15-year employee of the USDA committed to agriculture) that brought incredible value to the gathering.
3- more time for meaningful engagement for a farmer like me. It is difficult to connect with healthcare professionals, academics, philanthropy when the overwhelming feeling a farmer gets when in conversation with these folks is "this person I am talking with does not see me as a partner in this work and is not really listening to me." Americans do not value food or the people who grow it and although I am sure that there would be pushback on this comment, it is evident in the lack of understanding of the true cost of growing food and systemic efforts to keep it "affordable" at the economic, physical and emotional expense of the grower and our natural resources.
4- more small and mid-scale farms. Industrial ag should not be the future of FIM. Regenerative sustainable farming that cultivates soil health, growing our fruits and vegetables, should be prioritized. Local food systems and local food production should be prioritized.
5- hold additional convenings where there is an opportunity to visit small and mid-size farms to understand the challenges and support needed for growers.
6- more of an emphasis on the holistic interconnectedness across systems and a region-- urban and rural people and environments; housing; healthcare; transportation; education; all of these determine wellbeing.  
7- a no phones policy.
rows.7. Were there topics or perspectives you: Please, more farmers perspectives, especially around the understanding of beginning farmer development, land transition, and farm viability. FIM, if implemented to support local farmers and food systems, and prioritizing local institutional food procurement has the power to catalyze improved health and economic outcomes for regional communities, as well as healing our environment.

rows.5. Which session or moment was most valu:
I said my number one first piece of feedback would be the food - THANK YOU thank you thank you for the incredible food! You have ruined all other conference catering for me. As someone with weird food allergies, I felt completely comfortable eating, and not only that, but it was healthy and delicious - you actually walked the walk when it comes to food is medicine. A huge round of a applause.

Back to the original question. The SESSIONS where I took the most notes (my metric for most valuable) were the breakout on tailoring for specialized populations (day 1), the food is more than medicine morning session (day 2), and the human-centered design breakout session (day 2). The MOMENTS that were most valuable were the times I could connect with the speakers for a minute or two after their sessions to ask follow-up questions (I do understand the efficiency of keeping the conference moving by not having the audience questions) and I was able to get one-on-one advice (and took copious notes) for real issues affecting our FIM work and how to move forward, as well as the networking time to connect with others so we can get out of our own silos and see that we aren't alone in FIM work in our disparate areas.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro:
Honestly? It was a terrific conference. These are wishes, rather than 'the conference was terrible and needs these ideas.' 

I imagine keeping this to a packed two days was a factor of having advocacy day on Wednesday. That being said - having this over 2.5 days would have allowed for a bit more Q&A time, a little more break time between sessions (I'm sure you noticed that after speakers, everyone wanted to reflect with their tablemates, and when we had back to back speakers it was really hard to get the audience's attention again). 

Another idea - for those of us who are not in the closed door sessions and would really benefit from learning from the power players, would those with a lot of funding and experiences and resources and contacts be willing to do a speed dating or even 'come meet a rock star of FIM' session? We can't hope to compete funding-wise, and we don't necessarily need or want to, but perhaps we could learn from you or your connections so we could advance our own work in our small ways. All boats rise together, and all that. You all are in contact with each other, and behind closed doors you will continue to be so. That's fine. But having one listening ear who could suggest an idea or a paper to read or a connection at a regional center who could open a door could be a lifeline for a small philanthropy-funded program run by a handful of volunteers at a clinic that unfortunately do not have access to a swath of RDs and patient navigators. We're a drop in the bucket of FIM, but for our patients we're a big deal, so how do we best learn from those who have a lot of experience to share?
rows.7. Were there topics or perspectives you: There were a handful of 'integrating FIM into clinical care' sessions, but none of them actually addressed how a day to day PCP (a) identifies a person who could benefit from FIM or has food / nutrition insecurity, (b) brings the issue up with the patient, (c) connects them with resources, and (d) creates a FIM / food + nutrition security program within their own clinic. There were plenty of discussions on each side - this is who needs it, and these are the outcomes, but nothing about the clinicians who are already in practice and will not benefit from the proposed nutrition education in med school and who don't know how to talk to patients about food and who don't really have access to RDs or even know what food resources exist. And, for those who are just regular ol' PCPs and work in a clinic that doesn't screen or does not have a FIM / food+nutriton security program, what can they do to get one started? This is a HUGE issue, especially in areas of the country that are not the east coast where a lot of the funding and research happens, and based on the session titles I had high hopes. That's the one thing I am leaving the conference missing, and could have really used to make a demonstrable impact on our day to day workflow.

rows.22. Is there anything else you’d like to:
I will start by acknowledging the place (DC), the likely behind the scenes pressure given the federal speakers, and the current political environment in which we exist and the myriad consequences to funding that could occur. I can only imagine that the organizers, speakers, and panelists were given clear warnings about what they were and were not allowed to say. I also applaud the concept of the bipartisan panel; regardless of your party affiliation, this is something we can all get behind. 

That being said...this was an intelligent audience.  I don't know that you had a choice - but the inclusion of Dr. Oz in the midst of Medicaid and SNAP cuts was baffling at best and insulting at worst, and the hypocrisy of his comments about commitments to funding were not the best way to start the conference. I can believe, but am disappointed, that it took until the second day and a non-federal or academic speaker who wasn't afraid to compromise funding to acknowledge on stage the political situation in which we find ourselves. I felt terribly for Dr. Bremer, having to say that the NIH is open and has funding in the midst of the OMB rewrite. He had to know that we know the administration has made this all but impossible for most of us. While I admire Senator Marshall for coming to the session knowing the room was likely leaning more liberal and the bipartisan panel started well, it devolved quickly, and if his intention was to 'convert,' this was not the way to do it. Although I don't care for using this word, it was really triggering with all of the other administration's violence in various cities and states this year. To come into a room full of RDs and say the flipping of the food pyramid was a great idea, education doesn't work... We should absolutely not exist in an echo chamber, and we should absolutely work with and hear from people with whom we disagree. What would have been more useful is to hear from Republicans who could say 'I acknowledge we disagree on xyz, but we do agree on abc, so let's work together' - not Republicans who vociferously espouse the administration's agenda in an argumentative fashion - because we need to know what do agree on and how to work together. I am related to conservative Republicans who voted for the current president with whom I can have wonderful and thoughtful conversations on social issues, so I know it can happen. 

It felt...dishonest to talk about scaling up FIM when we're all scaling down or losing programs and jobs. I don't have a fix for this. I don't know what pressure you all were under. I imagine this sort of thing won't go away for a few years. But the tension, the fear, it was noticeable. Just an observation. 

Besides this - a fantastic conference that was long overdue, and thank you so much.

rows.5. Which session or moment was most valu: The way you centered patient voice and participants - hard to pick a favorite moment among those brave speakers. So grounding in why and for whom we are all doing this hard work.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro: I felt some of the panels bordered on "rah rah" or "preaching to the choir" - the panels where policy makers spoke to real concerns or needs were good, and some of the really practical / tactical issues that I wish everyone had debated or wrestled with were only covered in the breakout sessions (perhaps by design).  Everyone flipped out over the MAHA guy's insensitive comment but honestly that is what we are all up against and needed to work with/around for the next 3 years so it was super important to have happened!  It doesn't help if we get all these people in a room just to agree we're great for trying. I recommend having provocative discussion, debate, or tactical considerations as much as possible on the main stage.

I would like to see that content, or stuff like it, on more of the main stage / full audience.

rows.5. Which session or moment was most valu: The Monday afternoon break out group focused on client data and tech systems. This provided the most technical and nitty gritty information that directly connects to the work I need to do next to support FIM program development across Virginia. It provided systems thinking, tech platform considerations, program design, and client-centered/UCD approaches that I'll use in my work.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro:
The agenda was too full. I would have liked more time to connect with folks at my table in the Independence Hall space between speakers/give folks a chance to go to the bathroom or get water.

I also thought the morning sessions were too much sitting in one place. A morning breakout would be preferred to get more into the weeds and move a bit.
rows.7. Were there topics or perspectives you:
MCOs were largely absent. I think they need to be a larger part of the conversation. I think FIM work will need to be attractive and paid for by a variety of payers in the years ahead. We can't put all our eggs in the Medicaid basket. The MCO voice is needed. Our Virginia FIM program is aiming to be payer agnostic for this reason, and I would have loved hearing MCO voices that can help us understand paths toward sustainable funding partnership.

I also would have loved to hear more from the hosts/leaders in this space: CHLPI and FIMC especially. What did they learn in the past year that could support us/this work in the year ahead?

Which session or moment was most valu: I really appreciated the clinical sessions which talked about creating the value proposition behind Food Is Medicine because organizations are consistently focused on ROI and it was helpful to hear other thought leaders discuss their approach to identifying value and ROI. I also really appreciated the invitation to have various congressional and senate representatives at this conference so they could understand the need and importance of pushing Food as Medicine.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro:
There are a few suggestions that I'd make for a future FIMCON:
1. Create a little break between speakers. The back-to-back sessions had too much interruption as people stepped out to use the restroom or take a break.
2. Create a space for participants to ask questions. We all come to these forums to learn and it would have been better to have opportunities to connect with the speakers with questions. While I appreciated all of the sharing from the speakers, it was hard to find people at the break to ask any additional questions.
3. I really liked the breakout sessions to provide additional variety in addition to the keynote and mainstage speakers.
rows.7. Were there topics or perspectives you: When we talk about Food as Medicine, I believe the focus is around the provision of food to patients to help them with their health. I would like to see more discussion on the broader context of Food as Medicine that extends beyond the provision of food and includes community organizations working with communities on nutrition education and healthy eating behaviors, possibly ways that organizations are addressing food deserts, and other actions to improve access to more affordable healthy food since it's not sustainable for health care organizations to pay for food for life. One speaker stuck with me when he said that it was unreasonable to expect health care to pay for everything. I'd also like to see more research and clinical outcomes on FIM research.

rows.5. Which session or moment was most valu: Loved hearing patient, community, and food system perspectives.  Liked practical and research tracks.
rows.6. What would you most like to see impro: More evidence-based nutrition in FIM; some of the concepts presented did not have a strong evidence base. Findhelp and other social health access referral platforms should be added as a seamless tool to integrate clinical and community services to improve access for patients.  Suggest alternating practical and research tracks so that we can learn from each other rather than remaining in silos.  Agree that technology is essential but there was too much focus on AI at the individual patient level, even suggesting that some AI B2B products could replace dietitians in delivering precision medicine.  This is an unfortunate turn for FIM when nutrition is finally at the tipping point. Fortune 500 CFOs indicate that they are not seeing ROI on AI investments; FIM should not over prioritize this as a strategy.
rows.7. Were there topics or perspectives you: FIM efforts that leverage government programs and MTGs were underrepresented. Similarly, lifestyle medicine and doctoral trained RDNs were underrepresented.  Some of the concepts were presented as novel, but are theoretically grounded in nutrition science, sociology, and lifelong learning; this suggests that these perspectives were not well-represented.

I was one of the few people coming from outside of the US into the conference. I think it's highly important that the conference did have a US focus as you are the most advanced country in the world in this space. I think by being so specific it served your community in the best way.

It could be cool to have a small side panel about global FIM implementation, just to show also your local community how important their commitment to FIM and innovation is to us. It could also maybe inform some of the researchers on the importance of their design of their research in terms of thinking of scaling up and replication of findings outside of the US perspective.

I was glad that, on the 2nd day, finally the topic of SNAP cuts came up. It felt like an weird gap that no one mentioned the cuts to existing food security programs that our patients rely on. I wish there was a session on how healthcare can partner with food pantries to achieve FIM and food security goals. Leveraging the existing emergency food system and investing in their capacity to deliver FIM is a huge opportunity.

I would have appreciated more program manager level discussions about what works, not all top leaders.
